---
title: "Week 8 Homework"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
---

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
What is Reve's Puzzle? Describe it, and describe the Frame-Stewart
Algorithm in general and using an example.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Use generating functions to solve:\
a. $a_n=6a_{n-1}-8a_{n-2}+3$ with $a_0=1$ and $a_1=0$\
b. $a_n=3a_{n-1}+4^{n-1}$ and $a_0=1$\
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Use generating functions to solve:\
a. $a_n=\frac{1}{2}a_{n-1}+1$ with $a_1=1$\
b. $b_n=4b_{n-1}-3b_{n-2}+2^n$ with $b_1=1$ and $b_2=11$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Find the closed form solution to $a_n=a_{n-1}+2(n-1)$ with $a_1=2$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Solve the following recurrence relations:\
Find a recurrence relation for the number, $t_n$ of length $n$ ternary
strings with no two consecutive 1s. What are the initial conditions?
What is the generating function for $t_n$?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Solve the following recurrence relations:\
Find a recurrence relation for the number, $d_n$ of length $n$ ternary
strings with no two consecutive 1s or no two consecutive 2s. What are
the initial conditions? What is the generating function for $d_n$?
(Though very similar to the previous problem, this version is much
harder. Hint: The solution is not $d_n=d_{n-1}+2d_{n-2}$, that is only
part of the solution.)
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Consider tiling a $2 \times n$ board with $2 \times 1$ and $2 \times 2$
tiles.\
a. Let $d_n$ count the number of tilings of where the $2 \times 1$ can
only be positioned vertically and $2 \times 1$ cannot be adjacent. Find
a recursive relationship for $d_n$ and find initial conditions for
$d_n$.\
b. Let $g_n$ count the number of tilings of where the $2 \times 1$ can
only be positioned vertically and $2 \times 2$ cannot be adjacent. Find
a recursive relationship for $g_n$ and find initial conditions for
$g_n$.\
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Let $g_n$ count the number of subsets of $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$
containing no two consecutive integers. For example, $g_2=3$, the empty
set, $\{1\}$, and $\{2\}$. Find a recurrence relationship and generating
function for $g_n$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Solve the recurrence relation $a_n=2a_{n-1}-a_{n-2}$. Find the solution
when $a_0=1$ and $a_1=2$.\
What do the initial terms need to be in order for $a_9=30$?\
For which $x$ are there initial terms which make $a_9=x$?\
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Consider the recurrence relation $a_n=4a_{n-1}-4a_{n-2}$. Find the
general solution to the recurrence relation (beware the repeated root).\
Find the solution when $a_0=1$ and $a_1=2$.\
Find the solution when $a_0=1$ and $a_1=8$.\
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Define the Lucas numbers as $L_n=L_{n-1}+L_{n-2}$ with $L_1=1$ and
$L_2=3$. What is the generating function for this sequence? Find a
closed from solution for $L_n$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise"}
Let $f_n=f_{n-1}+f_{n-2}$ with $f_0=0$ and $f_1=1$. Define
$F_n=F_{n-1}+F_{n-2}+ f_n$ for $n \ge 1$. Express $F_n$ in terms $f_n$
and $f_{n+1}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
Consider the sequence $f_n=f_{n-1}+f_{n-2}$ with $f_0=0$ and $f_1=1$.
Prove $f_0+f_1+f_2+f_3 +\cdots f_n=f_{n+2}-1$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
Consider the sequence $d_n=(n-1)(d_{n-1}+d_{n-2})$ with $d_1=0$ and
$d_2=1$. Prove that $d_n>(n-1)!$ for $n\ge 4$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
Prove $\sum_{k=0}^n \binom{n}{k}4^k=5^n$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Suppose that a particular real number $x$ has the property
that $x+\frac{1}{x}$ is an integer. Prove that $x^n+\frac{1}{x^n}$is an
integer for all natural numbers $n$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Let $T_n$ be the triangular numbers defined by
$T_n=T_{n-1}+n$ with $T_0=0$ and $n$, $p$, and $q$ be positive integers.
Prove that if $T_n=pq$ then $T_{n+p+q}=T_{n+p}+T_{n+q}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Let $T_n$ be the triangular numbers defined by
$T_n=T_{n-1}+n$ with $T_0=0$ and $n$, $p$, and $q$ be positive integers.
Prove that if $T_{n+p+q}=T_{n+p}+T_{n+q}$ then $T_n=pq$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Consider the sequence $f_n=f_{n-1}+f_{n-2}$ with $f_1=1$ and
$f_2=2$. Calculate $f_1+f_3+f_5+f_7 +\cdots +f_{2n-1}$ for small $n$ to
create a hypothesis for the sum of the first $n$ odd indexed Fibonacci
numbers, then prove this formula.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Consider the sequence $f_n=f_{n-1}+f_{n-2}$ with $f_1=1$ and
$f_2=2$. Find (like the preceding problem) and prove formulas for:\
$f_0+f_2+f_4+f_6 +\cdots f_{2n}$\
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Consider the sequence $f_n=f_{n-1}+f_{n-2}$ with $f_1=1$ and
$f_2=2$. Find (like the preceding problem) and prove formulas for:\
$f_1-f_2+f_3-f_4 +\cdots (-1)^{n-1}f_{n}$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Consider the sequence $d_n=2d_{n-1}+d_{n-2}$ with $d_0=1$ and
$d_1=3$. Prove $$d_n=\sum_{i\ge0}^{}2^i\binom{n+1}{2i}$$ is a solution
to this recurrence relationship.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Using the definition for
$\binom{n}{k} =\frac{n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots (n-k+1)}{k!}$ when $n<0$, prove
$$\binom{-1/2}{n}=\frac{\binom{2n}{n}}{(-4)^n}$$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ Let $F_n$ be the Fibonacci sequence with $F_1=1$ and $F_2=1$.
Prove $F_n \leq (\frac{5}{3})^n$ for all positive integers.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Proof"}
$\bigstar$ The indicated diagonals of Pascal's triangle sum to Fibonacci
numbers. Prove this patterns continues forever.

<figure id="fig:my_figure" data-latex-placement="htbp">
<img src="Pascal and Fibonacci" style="width:80.0%" />
<figcaption>Fibonacci and Pascal</figcaption>
</figure>
:::
